refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health across all network segments.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Provide bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.2: Provide bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.3: Detect link degradation or loss and report status to Safety Services within [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.4: Maintain link availability through redundancy, failover, or network diversity mechanisms

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single dedicated wireless link (cellular or proprietary) with no redundancy
DP-B: Dual independent wireless links (e.g., cellular + satellite or dual cellular carriers) with automatic failover
DP-C: Hybrid wired-wireless architecture with fallback to lower-bandwidth link on primary failure
DP-D: Mesh network with multi-hop relay capability to ensure coverage and redundancy
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent wireless links with automatic failover and link health monitoring. Primary link uses commercial cellular (LTE/5G); secondary link uses satellite or alternative carrier. Automatic switchover triggered by link quality metrics (signal strength, packet loss rate, latency). Both links feed into a link aggregation layer that presents unified Telemetry and Tasking interfaces to consumers.

potential_sources: DP-B couples teleoperation availability to network infrastructure (cellular + satellite providers); requires SLA agreements and coverage verification.
DP-B couples to vehicle antenna design and RF environment; poor antenna placement or RF interference can degrade both links simultaneously.
DP-B couples to Safety Services for link-loss detection and failsafe behavior; must ensure Safety Services can act on link-down events within acceptable time.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via explicit SLA contracts, RF site surveys, and safety-critical link monitoring. No circular dependencies detected. DP-B is decoupled from specific vehicle propulsion or task execution logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual independent links can be procured and integrated within cost/schedule constraints.
Assumption: Satellite link latency (typically 500–1000 ms) is acceptable for teleoperation; if not, DP-B must be revised to use only terrestrial links with geographic redundancy.

Risk: Simultaneous failure of both links due to common-cause event (e.g., RF jamming, solar storm affecting satellite). Mitigation: failsafe behavior in Safety Services (e.g., vehicle enters safe state on dual-link loss).

Risk: Failover latency during link switchover may exceed operator tolerance. Mitigation: measure and validate switchover time; consider link aggregation (parallel transmission) instead of pure failover if latency is critical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify customer's latency tolerance and coverage requirements. If latency is critical (< 200 ms round-trip), revise DP-B to use dual terrestrial links (e.g., two cellular carriers or cellular + private LTE) instead of satellite. If coverage in remote areas is paramount, satellite is justified despite latency. Recommend explicit SLA targets for each link provider.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-link architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and the unified Telemetry/Tasking interface. It abstracts away the underlying network complexity (cellular, satellite, failover) from the Vehicle and Safety Services. Includes link aggregation layer, link state machine, and failover controller.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands from tele-operator (or autonomous planner) to vehicle. Teleoperation module receives Tasking commands and routes them over the active link to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state, sensor data, and link health status from Teleoperation module to tele-operator console and Safety Services. Includes link availability metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety signals: Teleoperation reports link-loss events to Safety Services; Safety Services may command failsafe actions (e.g., stop vehicle) that Teleoperation must enforce or relay to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network interfaces (cellular modem, satellite transceiver, network stack). Teleoperation module manages these interfaces, monitors their health, and switches between them.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of dual-link failover, link health monitoring, and link aggregation. All logic for achieving link availability resides here. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cross the boundary. High cohesion.

Safety Services should NOT own link availability logic; it should own failsafe response to link loss. If link availability logic is split between Teleoperation and Safety Services, diffusion increases (e.g., link monitoring in both modules, conflicting failover decisions). Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle module should not own teleoperation link logic; it should own vehicle control and sensor fusion. Placing link availability in Vehicle creates tight coupling to network infrastructure and diffuses teleoperation concerns across vehicle firmware. Not recommended.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own detailed link failover logic. Assigning to NGHE Solution would require further decomposition and would not clarify ownership. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-B (dual-link failover architecture). It has high cohesion (all link availability logic is contained), low diffusion (interfaces are well-defined), and clear responsibility boundaries. Link health monitoring, failover logic, and link aggregation are intrinsic to teleoperation and should not be scattered across Vehicle or Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it also owns latency optimization, compression, or encryption. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (link manager, failover controller, codec layer) at the next level.

Risk: Network Link interface is external and depends on third-party providers (cellular, satellite). If providers fail to meet SLA, Teleoperation cannot compensate. Mitigation: explicit SLA contracts and fallback procedures documented in Safety Services.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services' failsafe logic. If Safety Services changes its response to link loss, Teleoperation must adapt. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert protocol as a stable contract; version it explicitly.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module owns the dual-link failover logic and that Vehicle module does NOT duplicate link monitoring. Ensure SafetyAlert interface is formally specified (message format, timing, semantics) to prevent coupling surprises. Consider adding a sub-module for link health monitoring (separate from failover logic) if complexity warrants.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual independent links with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and feasible. Dual links with automatic failover are a proven pattern for high-availability systems. No independence violations: the DP does not over-constrain the design (e.g., it does not mandate specific vendors or frequencies). The DP is decoupled from vehicle propulsion, task execution, and operator interface design.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If link failover logic is also implemented in Vehicle firmware (e.g., vehicle detects link loss and enters safe state), then link availability logic is split between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Mitigation: Vehicle should NOT implement link failover; it should only respond to SafetyAlert commands from Safety Services.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services implements its own link health monitoring (separate from Teleoperation), then link state is duplicated. Mitigation: Safety Services should consume link health via Telemetry interface from Teleoperation; it should not independently monitor links.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains DP-B. All dual-link logic, failover state machine, and link aggregation are internal to Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not leak implementation details. Ownership is clear: Teleoperation team owns link availability; Vehicle team owns vehicle control; Safety Services team owns failsafe response.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 is feasible: instrument Teleoperation module to log link state (up/down) at high frequency (e.g., 1 Hz); calculate availability as (time_link_up / total_time) over evaluation period. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is measurable and objective. Requires test environment with controlled link failures (e.g., RF shielding, network emulation) to validate failover behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 availability. Sub-allocations to dual-link providers (cellular, satellite) are via SLA contracts. Sub-allocations to Safety Services (failsafe on link loss) are via SafetyAlert interface. No ambiguity about who owns what.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) to include latency and jitter targets. Current sub-FRs are incomplete without timing constraints.
Specify SafetyAlert protocol (message format, timing, error handling) to prevent misinterpretation between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Define link health metrics (signal strength, packet loss rate, latency) that trigger failover. Recommend thresholds be configurable per deployment.
Confirm that Vehicle module does NOT implement link monitoring or failover; it should only respond to SafetyAlert commands.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs for command uplink, telemetry downlink, link detection, and redundancy mechanisms. DP-B (dual-link failover) is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) must be refined with latency, jitter, and detection time targets before proceeding to detailed design. Additionally, the SafetyAlert interface contract must be formalized to ensure Safety Services and Teleoperation are synchronized on failsafe behavior.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Provide bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and packet loss rate ≤ [TBD] %
FR-NGHE-010.2: Provide bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and jitter ≤ [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.3: Detect link degradation or loss and report status to Safety Services within [TBD] ms; trigger failover to secondary link within [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.4: Maintain link availability through dual independent wireless links (primary: cellular; secondary: satellite or alternative carrier) with automatic failover on primary link failure

revise.why: Sub-FRs lack timing constraints (latency, jitter, detection time, failover time). These constraints are critical for safety-critical teleoperation and must be specified before DP selection at the sub-level. Recommend stakeholder workshop (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Manager) to define acceptable latency and failover time.

assumptions: Dual independent wireless links (cellular + satellite) can be procured and integrated within cost/schedule.
Satellite link latency (500–1000 ms) is acceptable for teleoperation; if not, DP-B must be revised to use only terrestrial links.
Safety Services will implement failsafe behavior (e.g., vehicle enters safe state) on dual-link loss; Teleoperation does not need to implement its own failsafe.
Vehicle module will NOT implement link monitoring or failover; it will only respond to SafetyAlert commands from Safety Services.
Tele-operator can tolerate failover latency of [TBD] ms (to be defined); if latency is critical, link aggregation (parallel transmission) may be required instead of pure failover.
open_risks: Simultaneous failure of both links due to common-cause event (RF jamming, solar storm, provider outage). Mitigation: failsafe behavior in Safety Services; explicit SLA contracts with providers.
Failover latency during link switchover may exceed operator tolerance. Mitigation: measure and validate switchover time; consider link aggregation if latency is critical.
Satellite link availability may be degraded in certain geographic regions or weather conditions. Mitigation: RF site survey; fallback to cellular-only in areas where satellite is unreliable.

SafetyAlert interface contract is not yet formalized; risk of misinterpretation between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert protocol (message format, timing, error handling) before detailed design.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined latency tolerance for teleoperation (round-trip command-to-response time).
Customer-defined failover time tolerance (maximum time vehicle can be without command before entering safe state).
RF site survey results for primary deployment area (cellular coverage, satellite visibility, interference).
SLA agreements with cellular and satellite providers (availability, latency, support response time).
Formal specification of SafetyAlert interface (message format, timing, error handling, state machine).
Test plan for link failover validation (RF shielding, network emulation, failover latency measurement).
